name: Kenji Yamamoto
reasoning: B solves the real failure mode, not just the surface inefficiency. Time saved on note-taking is wasted if decisions evaporate the moment people leave the call — A automates capture but says nothing about what happens after the meeting ends, which is exactly where projects derail. C is directionally interesting but feels like a sales-team tool; I'm running cross-functional delivery squads, not a pipeline review. Lost follow-ups and quietly forgotten decisions are the actual bottleneck, and B directly targets that.

name: Masahiro Ito
reasoning: Follow-through is the actual problem. Decisions get made in meetings, then nothing happens — we have that in manufacturing too. Option B at least addresses something real. Option A is just 'the machine takes notes for you,' which I don't trust. Option C sounds like something the sales department wants, not the factory floor.

name: Yuki Tanabe
reasoning: Honestly, B is the one that actually solves my real problem. The issue at FlowBoard isn't sitting in meetings — it's what happens after the Zoom ends. Decisions get made, everyone nods, and then two weeks later half the team doesn't even remember what was agreed or who owns what. A is fine but 'save time on admin' is basically marketing boilerplate at this point, every tool in the category says that. C feels like something a VP of Sales at a 300-person company wants so they can monitor their reps — not useful for a 12-person startup where the COO is also doing customer calls herself. B is the accountability layer I actually need.

name: Rina Ogawa
reasoning: The pain I feel most keenly is exactly what Concept B describes — a meeting concludes, everyone returns to their desks, and within a week the decisions we spent an hour reaching have been quietly reinterpreted or simply forgotten. I am often the one manually tracking those loose ends in my Hobonichi, which is not a sustainable system when I am managing a team. Concept A is tempting for the note-transcription burden alone, but an automatic recording function in a bank environment raises immediate questions about information security compliance that would require our IT department's review before I could even table the idea — I cannot assume that hurdle away. Concept C reads as a tool for sales teams tracking client pipelines, which is not my situation in corporate planning, so it felt the least relevant to my actual problem.

name: Takeshi Morimoto
reasoning: Of the three, B is the least objectionable — it focuses on structured outputs like decisions and follow-up items rather than emphasizing the recording and storage of raw meeting audio, which is where my primary concerns would sit. At a consulting firm, meeting content is essentially client-confidential data, and any tool touching it needs to clear a security review before the productivity conversation even begins. A reads like a commodity pitch with no differentiation, and C's framing around 'manager visibility into customer calls' immediately raises questions about who else can access that data and where it's being routed — CRM integrations typically mean another data residency problem. That said, I want to be clear: none of these concepts tells me anything I actually need to know to make a procurement decision.

name: Ayaka Nishimura
reasoning: Okay so A is tempting because honestly I hate being the person furiously typing notes while also trying to actively participate in a client session — that's like the worst combo. But when I really think about it, my actual problem isn't capturing stuff in the moment, it's what happens *after* the call when I'm juggling three other projects and a follow-up slips through. B speaks directly to that. C felt very... enterprise-y? 'Manager visibility into pipeline' — that's not my world at all, I'm a solo freelancer, there's no manager.

name: Sachiko Harada
reasoning: In our work, if I tell a client I'll follow up on something by the end of the month and then it slips through — that's not just an inconvenience, that's a trust problem, maybe even a liability. So the follow-through concept is the one that solves a real pain point for us. Option A is nice, I'm not against saving time on summaries, but honestly the bottleneck in my office isn't writing notes — it's making sure nobody drops the ball on what was promised. Option C sounds like something for a company with a proper sales team and a CRM system, which we don't have — we're six people doing tax work.

name: Shota Kimura
reasoning: Honestly, A hits exactly where it hurts for me — I spend like two hours every night in my apartment writing up visit reports after a full day of doctor rounds, and it's exhausting. If this thing can auto-generate summaries and capture action items on its own, that's basically giving me back my evenings. B sounds useful too but it feels more like a project management tool, and C — the manager visibility angle actually made me a little nervous, like I'd be getting watched more closely even when I'm stuck waiting 45 minutes in a clinic lobby. A keeps it simple: less admin, more time for actual selling.

name: Noboru Watanabe
reasoning: Option B addresses what I regard as the essential purpose of a meeting record: ensuring that decisions made and responsibilities assigned are faithfully preserved and followed through. Option C appears to be oriented toward sales pipeline management in the private sector — terminology such as 'CRM' and 'customer calls' has little bearing on the work of a prefectural government division. Option A's emphasis on automation and speed gives me pause, as the accuracy of a meeting record is not something that should be subordinated to efficiency; in our context, these records carry administrative and, at times, legal weight.

name: Haruka Fujimoto
reasoning: Look, Concept A is the obvious pitch — 'save time on minutes' — but that's basically table stakes at this point; any decent AI meeting tool checks that box. What actually creates organizational drag isn't the note-taking itself, it's the execution gap that opens up after the meeting ends: commitments that get quietly dropped, decisions nobody can reconstruct two weeks later, follow-ups that live in someone's inbox and die there. Concept B targets that specific failure mode, and in the Japanese enterprise context where consensus is built slowly across many meetings, losing track of what was decided and who owns what is genuinely costly. For the internal business case I'd need to build, 'measurably improved follow-through rate' is a more defensible ROI narrative to executive sponsors than 'we saved hours on admin.' Concept C is interesting but it's optimized for sales management visibility — too narrow for the cross-functional deployment scale I'm thinking about.
